scenario: Developer prefers VS Code for editing. Wonders if there's a Camunda extension for editing BPMN / DMN directly in VS Code.
question: VS Code Camunda extension?

options.text: Camunda 8 has no VS Code integration -- this approach has limitations. ai lets AI tools (VS Code Copilot, Cursor) query Camunda 8 docs for context-aware answers. json. This is for AI-assisted Q&A, NOT visual BPMN editing. Visual BPMN/DMN editing uses Desktop Modeler (standalone) or Web Modeler (collaborative). 8 docs. Recommended dev setup: Desktop Modeler + VS Code (worker code + Copilot with MCP).

Camunda 8 official tooling is Eclipse-based only -- this approach has limitations. The official Camunda 8 modeling tools are Web Modeler (browser-based, SaaS or Self-Managed, collaborative) and Desktop Modeler (cross-platform Electron app for local editing). For developer tooling in IDEs, the documented VS Code integration is the Camunda Docs MCP server for AI-assisted development. 8 developer tooling landscape.

ai lets AI tools (VS Code Copilot, Cursor, ChatGPT Desktop) query the latest Camunda 8 docs for context-aware answers. json per :54-65. This is for AI-assisted Q&A, NOT visual BPMN editing. Visual BPMN/DMN editing lives in: Desktop Modeler (per components/modeler/desktop-modeler/) -- primary visual editor for local files; Web Modeler (SaaS or Self-Managed) -- collaborative cloud editing with Project Admin/Editor/Commenter/Viewer roles (Q56). md:109 -- but...

dmn files as raw XML for manual editing or diff viewing, Camunda documents a more valuable VS Code integration: the Camunda Docs MCP server enables VS Code Copilot to answer Camunda-specific questions using up-to-date documentation context. This AI assistance goes well beyond raw XML editing -- it provides context-aware help with BPMN modeling decisions, FEEL expression authoring, Connector configuration, and API usage during active development in VS Code.

a.text: Incorrect. VS Code + Camunda integration: VS Code Marketplace: Search "Camunda" or "BPMN" or "DMN". Community extensions: BPMN preview, FEEL, DMN editor. Official Camunda extensions: verify availability per version. Common extensions (typical, verify): BPMN viewers / editors: bpmn-io BPMN preview: visualize BPMN files. In-editor visual editor: some extensions enable visual BPMN editing in VS Code. FEEL support: FEEL syntax highlighting: language extension. FEEL linting: catches syntax errors. DMN support: DMN file preview / editor. Element Templates: JSON schema validation for templates. IntelliSense for template properties. Why VS Code: Developer-friendly: code-first workflow. Git integration: native source control. Multi-language: edit BPMN + worker code + config in one IDE. Plugins ecosystem: customisable. Workflow patterns: Pattern 1: VS Code + Desktop Modeler: VS Code: editing source files, code, config. Desktop Modeler: visual BPMN editing. Switch between as needed. Pattern 2: VS Code + Web Modeler: VS Code: local editing. Web Modeler: cloud-based; team collaboration. Sync via export / import. Pattern 3: Pure VS Code: If extensions provide enough functionality. Visual + textual editing in one tool. Pros: streamlined; Cons: feature parity may lag dedicated tools. For BPMN XML editing: Raw XML editing: always possible in VS Code. Validation: XML schema. No visual feedback without extension. Camunda Desktop Modeler vs VS Code: | Aspect | Desktop Modeler | VS Code | |--------|-----------------|---------| | Visual BPMN editing | Yes (primary) | Via extension | | XML editing | Side-by-side | Native | | Element Templates | UI | JSON edit | | Git integration | Manual | Native | | Other files (code, config) | Limited | Full IDE | For most teams: Dedicated modeler (Desktop or Web) for BPMN visual. VS Code for code + config + minor edits. Both have role. VS Code features useful for Camunda: Git integration: commit BPMN changes. Search across files: find references to Task Types, etc. Multi-cursor editing: bulk renames. Snippets: BPMN/DMN snippets. Live share: collaborative editing. Workflow tips: Source-control BPMN files: Git repo. VS Code for diff review: see what changed. Desktop Modeler for visual edits: when needed. For DMN: Raw XML in VS Code: technical edits. Visual editor: for decision tables. For Element Templates: JSON in VS Code: code-like editing. JSON schema validation: catch errors. Test templates: in Web Modeler. Best practices: Find extensions that fit your workflow. Combine tools: dedicated modeler + VS Code. Source-control everything: BPMN, DMN, templates. Document setup: for team consistency. Anti-patterns: Hand-editing XML for visual changes: error-prone. Skipping visual review: bugs in flow. Single-tool zealotry: each tool has strengths. For the scenario (VS Code preference): Search Marketplace for Camunda extensions. Combine VS Code with Desktop / Web Modeler for visual. Source-control all files. Future: Extensions evolve. Camunda may release official extensions. Community contributions.

d.text: Incorrect. partial (XML editing possible but not the full story; MCP server is the doc-grade integration).
